plainMeaning: In the fifth vow I promised to cap how much I own across nine kinds of property, and now I confess every way I crossed those limits or grasped for more.
recitation: pāñcamuṁ parigraha parimāṇa vrata navavidha, khitta, ghara, haṭṭa, vāḍiya, kuviya, dhaṇā, dhanna, hiraṇṇa, suvaṇṇa ai-parimāṇā, duppaya, cauppaya-m-iya, navaviha pariggaha-vayanto. e pāñcamā parigraha parimāṇa vrata taṇāṁ pāñca atiyār śodhuṁ. khitta-vatthu ppamāṇāikkame, hiraṇṇa-suvaṇṇa ppamāṇāikkame, dhaṇā-dhanna ppamāṇāikkame, duppaya-cauppaya ppamāṇāikkame, kuviya ppamāṇāikkame. kṣetra-vāstu, hiraṇṇa, suvaṇṇa, dhana, dhānya, dvipada, catuṣpada, rāch-pīch-taṇo pramāṇātikrama kīdho hoy, athavā parigraha-taṇe kāraṇe moṭā ārambha kaṭha-marda māṇḍyā hoy. āhaḍḍ, dohaḍḍ dharyo hoy. āpaṇo parigraha putra pautrādika-taṇo, karī āpaṇe kāje aṇāvyo hoy, parigraha-pramāṇa līdhuṁ na hoy. laine paḍhiyuṁ na hoy, paḍhīne visāryuṁ hoy, visārīne sambhāryuṁ na hoy. aneruṁ e pāñcamā parigraha parimāṇa vrata viṣe.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pañcama 'fifth' + Guj. neuter -uṁ; from pañca 'five'.
wordByWord.notes: Marks the fifth aṇuvrata; recurs as pāñcamā below in the oblique.
Glossary term; here the object of the vow's limit.
Glossary term parigraha-parimāṇa: the self-set ceiling on holdings.
Heads the canonical nine categories of property listed next.
Prakrit form of the first property-head; respelled kṣetra in the gloss-line.
Corresponds to vāstu in the Tattvārtha list.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. haṭṭa = Skt. haṭṭa 'market'; cf. Hindi hāṭ.
wordByWord.notes: Immovable commercial premises.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. kuviya = Skt. kupya 'base metals; movable household stuff'.
wordByWord.notes: The fifth atichāra-category; glossed below as rāch-pīch.
Paired with dhana as dhana-dhānya.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. hiraṇṇa = Skt. hiraṇya, here conventionally 'silver'.
wordByWord.notes: In this pairing hiraṇya=silver, suvarṇa=gold.
Signals that the listed items are not to exceed their fixed amounts.
Traditionally male/female servants (dāsī-dāsa).

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. cauppaya = Skt. catuṣpada 'four-footed' + euphonic -m- + iya '(and) such'.
wordByWord.notes: -m- is a sandhi hiatus-breaker; iya gestures 'etc.' closing the list.
Prakrit doublet of navavidha above, restating the ninefold scope.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. pariggaha (= parigraha) + vaya (= vrata) + present-participle -nto 'being one who...'.
wordByWord.notes: Describes the votary as actively bounding his possessions.
Agrees with vrata in the genitive phrase.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. genitive postposition taṇo/taṇāṁ (cf. Apabhraṁśa taṇaya).
wordByWord.notes: Recurs as taṇe/taṇo below.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. atichāra; spelled atiyār (અતિયાર) throughout this book.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; the book's authoritative spelling with ય.
Refrain verb: simultaneously 'scrutinise' and 'purge'.
First canonical atichāra-pair.
Refrain repeated five times, once per atichāra-pair; the doubled pp- is sandhi-gemination.
Second atichāra-pair.
Third atichāra-pair.
Fourth atichāra-pair.
Fifth atichāra-category (repeated from the opening list).
Sanskritised gloss of khitta-vatthu, opening the Gujarati explanation.
Repeated in the gloss-list.
Repeated in the gloss-list.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. colloquial reduplicative rāch-pīch 'sundry utensils/stuff' + genitive taṇo.
wordByWord.notes: Vernacular rendering of kupya; rāch = household vessels/implements.
Sanskrit form of the Prakrit pamāṇāikkama refrain.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Guj. past participle of karavuṁ 'to do' (Skt. √kṛ).

wordByWord.notes: Recurs throughout: '...may have happened', framing tentative confession.

wordByWord.etymology: parigraha + Old Guj. oblique genitive taṇe.

wordByWord.gloss: by reason of, on account of

wordByWord.notes: Technical Jain ārambha: livelihood acts that destroy life.
Heavy, life-injuring labour driven by acquisitiveness.
Plural agreeing with ārambha (undertakings).

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt./Apabhr. āhaḍḍa, cf. Skt. ā-√hṛ 'to fetch, amass'.
wordByWord.notes: Paired alliteratively with dohaḍḍ; expresses grasping accumulation.

wordByWord.etymology: Apabhr. echo-word on āhaḍḍ (do- 'two/double'), intensifying jingle.
wordByWord.notes: Rhyming pair āhaḍḍ-dohaḍḍ = greedy heaping-up; verse-specific idiom (see notes).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pautra 'grandson' + ādika 'et cetera' + Guj. genitive taṇo.
wordByWord.notes: Property held in descendants' names yet effectively one's own.
Here 'reckoning (it) as...'.

wordByWord.etymology: Guj. causative of āvavuṁ 'to come' → aṇāvvuṁ 'to have fetched'.
wordByWord.notes: Acquiring through others to dodge one's own limit.
i.e. failed to formally set the limit.
Negates the preceding verb; recurs below.
Begins the take–study–forget–recall cadence.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt. paḍhai = Skt. √paṭh 'to read aloud, study'.

wordByWord.notes: The chain take→learn→forget→fail-to-recall pinpoints lapses of mindfulness.
Opens the sealing refrain abbreviated by the closing ૦.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. viṣaya 'sphere'; Guj. postposition viṣe 'in the matter of'.
Editorial cipher (zero/circle) marking an elided fixed refrain.
wordByWord.notes: Cues the reciter to supply '...je kāṁi sūkṣma-bādara atiyār huo hoy, te savi huṁ mane vacane kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.'

literalTranslation: Fifth, the possession-limiting vow, ninefold: field, house, shop, garden, household-goods, wealth, grain, silver, gold — (kept) not beyond measure — bipeds, quadrupeds and the like; (he being) one who limits the ninefold possession. I now examine the five transgressions of this fifth possession-limiting vow: in exceeding the measure of land-and-building, of silver-and-gold, of wealth-and-grain, of bipeds-and-quadrupeds, of sundry goods. If I have overstepped the limit of land-house, silver, gold, wealth, grain, bipeds, quadrupeds, and household chattels; or, for the sake of possessions, set going great injurious enterprises and harsh grinding toil; held to greedy hoarding and over-amassing; had my own possession — that of son, grandson and the like — fetched for my own use; not taken the possession-limit; or having taken it, not studied it, having studied it forgotten it, having forgotten it failed to recall it — and whatever other (lapse) regarding this fifth possession-limiting vow [may my wrong be undone].
idiomaticTranslation: The fifth vow is to set a ceiling on what I own across nine heads of property — land, house, shop, orchard, household goods, money, grain, silver, gold, servants, cattle and the rest — vowing never to hold more than my measure. Let me now search out the five ways I may have breached it: by exceeding my limit in land and building, in silver and gold, in money and grain, in servants and livestock, or in sundry chattels. If, for the sake of having more, I launched great harmful ventures and back-breaking toil; if I clung to greedy hoarding; if I had property registered to my son or grandson yet fetched it for my own use; if I never fixed my limit at all, or fixed it and never studied it, studied it and forgot it, forgot it and never brought it back to mind — for this and every other lapse in the fifth vow, may my wrongdoing be undone.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: After the four vows that guard life, truth, property and chastity, the confession turns to the root passion behind so much harm — greed for things — and audits it under the parigraha-parimāṇa vow. The verse first recites the canonical 'navavidha' inventory of property (khitta, ghara, haṭṭa, vāḍiya, kuviya, dhana, dhanna, hiraṇṇa, suvaṇṇa, duppaya, cauppaya), then quotes the five Prakrit atichāra-formulas built on the single refrain 'pamāṇāikkame' — exceeding the measure — paired item by item exactly as in Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.24. What distinguishes this verse is the unusually vivid medieval-Gujarati gloss that follows the formula. It refuses to let 'limiting possessions' stay abstract: it indicts the great injurious enterprises (moṭā ārambha) and 'harsh grinding toil' (kaṭha-marda) that acquisitiveness sets in motion — reminding the reciter that hoarding is never victimless but spins out violence (himsā) through one's livelihood. The jingling pair āhaḍḍ-dohaḍḍ caricatures the grasping, heaping reflex of the miser. Sharpest of all is the clause on property held in a son's or grandson's name yet 'fetched for one's own use' (āpaṇe kāje aṇāvyo): the verse closes the favourite loophole of evading one's vowed ceiling by titling assets to dependants. Finally the take–study–forget–recall cadence (līdhuṁ–paḍhiyuṁ–visāryuṁ–sambhāryuṁ) confesses a subtler failure: not breaking the limit, but never seriously fixing it, or fixing and then letting it lapse from memory — a lapse of the very mindfulness the vow exists to cultivate. The verse then seals, per the sūtra's fixed pattern, with the abbreviated micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ marked by ૦.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Parigraha in Jain doctrine is twofold — bāhya (external goods) and ābhyantara (inner attachment, the fourteen internal granthis) — and the decisive bondage is the inner mūrcchā, 'infatuation', which Umāsvāti defines as parigraha itself (Tattvārtha 7.12). The lay vow tackles the external by quantified self-limitation (parimāṇa), the householder's graded substitute for the muni's total aparigraha. This verse embodies the classical insight that possession is not morally inert: it drives ārambha — life-destroying enterprise — so the fifth vow is structurally tied back to the first (ahiṁsā). The loophole-clause on assets held in descendants' names shows the tradition policing bhāva (intention) over mere legal title: what one effectively controls and craves is one's parigraha regardless of whose name holds it, matching the canonical bhāva-atikrama. The closing memory-cadence reflects the doctrine that a vow demands sustained upayoga (mindful attention); to forget one's own limit is itself a transgression, because apramāda (vigilance) is the soul of vrata.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today can make this verse concrete: have I actually fixed a numeric ceiling on home, savings, gold, gadgets and the rest — or do I merely intend to 'live simply'? The verse insists the limit be set, written, periodically reviewed and remembered, not left vague. It also asks the modern householder to notice the harm-supply-chain behind acquisitions (ārambha, kaṭha-marda) and the familiar dodge of parking wealth in family members' names while still enjoying it. Practically, it invites an annual audit: re-affirm the limit, surrender or donate the surplus, and renew mindfulness rather than let the vow fade from memory.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.12 — mūrcchā parigrahaḥ (infatuation/attachment is the essence of possession)

Ratnakaraṇḍa-śrāvakācāra, parigraha-parimāṇa aṇuvrata and its atichāras

sources.title: Parigraha — definitions (Jainism section, incl. Tattvārtha 7.24 list of property-heads)
sources.consultedFor: Verifying the senses of parigraha and the kṣetra-vāstu/hiraṇya-suvarṇa/dhana-dhānya/dvipada-catuṣpada/kupya category-set.

sources.title: Aparigraha (Non-possession)
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal framing of parigraha/aparigraha and the bāhya–ābhyantara (external/internal) and mūrcchā analysis.

sources.title: Anuvrata (Śrāvaka / Jain lay vows)
sources.consultedFor: Placing parigraha-parimāṇa as the fifth aṇuvrata among the householder's vows and its standard transgressions.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (પંચ પ્રતિક્રમણ સૂત્ર – વિધિ સહિત), Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha Gaccha edition
sources.consultedFor: The verbatim Gujarati/Prakrit verse text, its atiyār spelling, and the sealing-refrain convention marked by ૦.

notes: (1) hiraṇya here is rendered 'silver' (paired against suvarṇa 'gold'), the usual reading in this dvandva, though hiraṇya can mean gold generally. (2) kuviya/kupya and its vernacular gloss rāch-pīch denote miscellaneous household metalware/chattels; 'rāch-pīch' is a Gujarati colloquialism, glossed by sense. (3) āhaḍḍ–dohaḍḍ is a rhyming Apabhraṁśa-flavoured echo-pair; āhaḍḍ (< ā-√hṛ 'amass') is fairly secure, but dohaḍḍ is largely an intensifying jingle and the precise lexical value is uncertain — read as 'greedy hoarding/over-amassing'. (4) kaṭha-marda 'harsh grinding toil' is interpreted from kaṭha 'hard' + mardana 'crushing'; a variant sense 'hard bargaining/oppression' is possible. (5) The classical list names dāsī-dāsa (servants) where this verse has duppaya 'bipeds' — equivalent. (6) The trailing ૦ is the book's abbreviation cipher for the fixed sealing refrain (sūkṣma-bādara ... micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ), not an erratum. (7) Per the preface, the pakkhī→cāumāsī→sāṁvatsarī substitution and the sūtra's overall arc are not repeated here.
